---
title: "Encrypted Mempool"
description: "Build and submit encrypted-mempool transactions using the Aptos TypeScript SDK 7.0+, covering authenticationKey, gas-floor, simulation, multi-agent, sponsored, and orderless flows."
sidebar:
  label: "Encrypted Mempool"
---

import { Aside, Steps } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Encrypted-mempool transactions wrap your executable payload so it is **encrypted at build time** using the fullnode's current **per-epoch encryption key** (batch threshold encryption on BLS12-381, with AES-GCM for the plaintext). The signing flow is the same as for plaintext transactions: **build → (optional) simulate on a plaintext twin → sign → submit → wait**. The difference is the `options` you pass to `aptos.transaction.build.simple` (or `build.multiAgent` / sponsored builds).

<Aside type="note">
  **Encrypted mempool** is the protocol-level name for this feature ([AIP-144](https://github.com/aptos-foundation/AIPs/blob/main/aips/aip-144-encrypted-mempool.md)); some product-level docs and announcements call it **encrypted pending transactions** — same feature.
</Aside>

Encrypted-mempool support shipped in **`@aptos-labs/ts-sdk` 7.0.0**. For background on what is protected and what may still be visible on the network, see the **[Encrypted Pending Transactions](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions)** guide.

<Aside type="note">
  In these examples, `aptos` is an instance of the [`Aptos`](https://aptos-labs.github.io/aptos-ts-sdk/) client and `sender` is an `Account` that will sign the transaction.
</Aside>

## Requirements

Encrypted-mempool support requires **`@aptos-labs/ts-sdk` ≥ 7.0.0**, which is **ESM-only** and targets **Node.js 22+**. Install with:

```bash
pnpm add @aptos-labs/ts-sdk@^7.0.0
# or: npm install @aptos-labs/ts-sdk@^7.0.0
```

Consumers must use `"type": "module"` / `import` syntax — CommonJS `require` is no longer supported. The SDK ships `nodenext` module resolution and exposes **per-feature sub-path exports** (for example `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/transaction`, `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/keyless`) for bundlers that benefit from tree-shaking.

## Two ways to call the SDK

The SDK supports both the familiar `Aptos` namespace class and a tree-shakeable namespace class loaded from a sub-path. Both expose the same encrypted-mempool surface; pick the style that matches your bundle goals.

```typescript
// Option A — Aptos namespace class (familiar, not tree-shakeable):
import { Aptos, AptosConfig, Network } from "@aptos-labs/ts-sdk";
const aptos = new Aptos(new AptosConfig({ network: Network.DEVNET }));

// Option B — Transaction sub-path (tree-shakeable, v7.0.0+):
import { Transaction, AptosConfig } from "@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/transaction";
import { Network } from "@aptos-labs/ts-sdk";
const transaction = new Transaction(new AptosConfig({ network: Network.DEVNET }));
```

All subsequent examples on this page use **Option A** for parity with the rest of the TS-SDK docs. To switch to Option B, substitute `transaction.build.simple(...)` for `aptos.transaction.build.simple(...)` and the equivalent for `sign`, `submit`, and `signAndSubmitTransaction`.

## Prerequisites

When you set `encrypted: true`, the SDK **requires** `authenticationKey` for the sender. It accepts either:

- an **`AccountPublicKey`** — the SDK derives the on-chain authentication key automatically via `.authKey()`, or
- a raw **32-byte hex string** (`HexInput`) — useful when you already have the auth key materialized.

For a standard `Account`, passing the signing public key directly is the simplest form:

```typescript
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey, // SDK derives the auth key via .authKey()
  },
});
```

The fullnode must return a non-empty **`encryption_key`** on ledger info. If the key is missing, building with `encrypted: true` throws and tells you the node does not support encrypted submission.

## Check whether the network supports encryption

Call **`aptos.getLedgerInfo()`** and inspect **`encryption_key`**. If it is missing or empty, do not offer encrypted submission against that endpoint.

```typescript
const ledgerInfo = await aptos.getLedgerInfo();
const supportsEncrypted = Boolean(ledgerInfo.encryption_key);
```

## Build, sign, and submit (single signer)

<Steps>
  1. **Build** with `encrypted: true` and `authenticationKey`.

     ```typescript
     const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
       sender: sender.accountAddress,
       data: {
         function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
         functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
       },
       options: {
         encrypted: true,
         authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
       },
     });
     ```

  2. **Simulate (optional)** using a plaintext build with the same `data` and gas-related `options`, but **without** `encrypted` or `authenticationKey`. Apply any gas adjustments, then **rebuild with `encrypted: true`** for submission. See [Simulation](#simulation-and-encrypted-payloads) below.

  3. **Sign and submit** as usual.

     ```typescript
     const pending = await aptos.signAndSubmitTransaction({
       signer: sender,
       transaction,
     });

     await aptos.waitForTransaction({ transactionHash: pending.hash });
     ```
</Steps>

## Gas-unit price floor

Encrypted submissions pay a minimum gas-unit price of **200 Octas/gas-unit** — twice the network base minimum — to cover validator decryption cost. The constant is exported by the SDK:

```typescript
// Re-exported from the main entry (works on any v7.0.x):
import { MIN_ENCRYPTED_TXN_GAS_UNIT_PRICE } from "@aptos-labs/ts-sdk";
```

How the floor is applied during `build.simple`:

- If `options.gasUnitPrice` is **unset** and the network estimate falls below 200, the SDK silently bumps the effective price to 200.
- If `options.gasUnitPrice` is **explicitly set below 200**, the SDK emits a `console.warn` and bumps the price to 200.
- Prices at or above 200 are unaffected.

Servers reject encrypted transactions priced below this floor, so the SDK never submits one. Account funding must cover `maxGasAmount × gasUnitPrice` (at least 200) at submit time.

## Simulation and encrypted payloads

`aptos.transaction.simulate.*` **rejects** transactions whose payload is already encrypted. The SDK throws before contacting the node with `ENCRYPTED_TRANSACTION_SIMULATION_NOT_SUPPORTED_MESSAGE`:

> Transaction simulation is not supported for encrypted payloads. Build a plaintext transaction with the same entry function and simulate it, then build with `options.encrypted` true for submission.

Workflow:

1. `build.simple({ ..., options: { /* no encrypted */ } })` — same function, arguments, gas hints.
2. `aptos.transaction.simulate.simple({ ... })` — tune `maxGasAmount` / `gasUnitPrice` from the result.
3. `build.simple({ ..., options: { encrypted: true, authenticationKey, ...gas from step 2 } })` — submit this build.

See [Simulating Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/simulating-transactions) for general simulation behavior.

## Multi-agent (encrypted)

Pass **`secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys`** with one entry per `secondarySignerAddresses` entry, in the **same order**. Each entry accepts an `AccountPublicKey` or a raw 32-byte hex string.

```typescript
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.multiAgent({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  secondarySignerAddresses: [secondary.accountAddress],
  data: {
    function: "0x<module>::<contract>::<multi_agent_entry>",
    functionArguments: [/* ... */],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys: [secondary.publicKey],
  },
});
```

The SDK rejects the build at validation time if the array length does not match `secondarySignerAddresses`. See [Multi-Agent Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/multi-agent-transactions).

## Fee payer (sponsored, encrypted)

When the fee payer address is **non-zero**, the SDK requires **`feePayerAuthenticationKey`**. AAD ordering is **sender → secondaries → fee payer** (matching `TransactionAuthenticator::all_signer_auth_keys` in `aptos-core`).

```typescript
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  withFeePayer: true,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey,
  },
});
```

Setting `feePayerAuthenticationKey` without a non-zero fee payer address (or vice versa) is a build-time error. See [Sponsoring Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/sponsoring-transactions).

## Orderless and encrypted

Pass **`replayProtectionNonce`** together with `encrypted: true`. The raw transaction uses orderless replay protection (`sequence_number = u64::MAX`) while the payload remains encrypted. See [Orderless Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/orderless-transactions).

```typescript
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    replayProtectionNonce: 12345n,
  },
});
```

## `claimedEntryFunction`

`claimed_entry_fun` is an **optional** field on the encrypted payload (aptos-core `EncryptedInner`). When present, it lets pre-decryption observers — sponsors, multisig owners, transaction filters — see the entry function's module (and optionally function name) before validators decrypt the payload. Validators verify the claim against the decrypted entry function and fail the transaction with `ClaimedEntryFunctionMismatch` if it doesn't match. The protocol never requires the field to be `Some`.

The SDK auto-populates it (with the real entry function being encrypted) when **either** of these is true:

- the transaction has a **non-zero fee payer** (sponsored), **or**
- the payload is **multisig** (`build.simple` with `multisigAddress`, or any inner payload carrying a multisig address).

For plain single-signer encrypted transactions, the SDK leaves the field `None` and `options.claimedEntryFunction` is ignored. **You typically never need to set this option** — it acts as an override on top of the SDK's auto-derived value, and is only respected in the fee-payer / multisig cases above.

### When the override is useful

The main reason to override is to **omit `functionName`**, exposing only the module to pre-decryption observers while keeping the called function private:

```typescript
options: {
  encrypted: true,
  authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
  feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey,
  // Reveal only the module; function name stays encrypted until decryption.
  claimedEntryFunction: {
    module: "0x1::aptos_account",
    // functionName intentionally omitted
  },
};
```

`ClaimedEntryFunction.fromEntryFunction(entryFunction)` is the default behavior and includes both module and function name; constructing the option manually (plain-object or SDK class form) lets you drop `functionName`.

```typescript
// Alternative class form (equivalent to the plain-object above):
claimedEntryFunction: new ClaimedEntryFunction(
  ModuleId.fromStr("0x1::aptos_account"),
  /* function */ undefined,
);
```

When the override **does** set a `functionName`, it must match the real entry function name exactly — the build throws (`assertClaimMatchesExecutable`) otherwise. The module field must always match. Setting `claimedEntryFunction` for a payload whose executable is not an entry function (e.g. a script) also throws.

## Keyless restriction

Encrypted transactions cannot be signed by **keyless** or **federated keyless** accounts. Signature malleability in those schemes would break payload binding (server-enforced in `aptos-core`). `signAndSubmitTransaction` throws **before submission** if the signer or the fee payer is a keyless account.

<Aside type="caution">
  A `MultiKeyAccount` wrapping a keyless inner signer is **not** caught by this client-side guard (detection is duck-typed via `isKeylessSigner`). Such a build still fails at the server, but the error surfaces later in the round-trip.
</Aside>

In v7.0.0 the keyless types — `KeylessAccount`, `FederatedKeylessAccount`, `EphemeralKeyPair`, and the poseidon utilities — moved out of the main entry into the **`@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/keyless`** sub-path. If you're migrating from \< 7.0.0, update those imports even though keyless accounts can't sign encrypted transactions:

```typescript
import { KeylessAccount, EphemeralKeyPair } from "@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/keyless";
```

## Multisig (encrypted)

Encrypted multisig **payloads** build correctly with this SDK (`build.simple` with `multisigAddress` in `data`), but server-side support is still rolling out. Submission may fail with:

- `FEATURE_UNDER_GATING` — encrypted submission gated off on the target network, or
- `"Encrypted transactions do not support multisig"` — server explicitly rejects the combination.

Treat both as rollout limitations; SDK behavior will not change when the gate flips.

## Reading encrypted transactions from the API

`TransactionPayloadResponse` gains an `EncryptedTransactionPayloadResponse` variant, discriminated on `encrypted_state`:

- `"encrypted"` — payload bytes only; no decrypted contents available.
- `"failed_decryption"` — validators could not decrypt this payload.
- `"decrypted"` — `decrypted_payload` contains the original entry function / script after on-chain execution.

```typescript
if (payload.type === "encrypted_payload") {
  switch (payload.encrypted_state) {
    case "encrypted":
    case "failed_decryption":
      // No plaintext; surface metadata (sender, gas, claimed_entry_fun).
      break;
    case "decrypted":
      // payload.decrypted_payload is available.
      break;
  }
}
```

`decryption_failure_reason` is reserved on the type but **not yet surfaced** by the REST API — treat it as optional.

## New exports (`@aptos-labs/ts-sdk` 7.0.0)

| Export                               | Purpose                                                                                                              | Import from                                                                   |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TransactionPayloadEncryptedPayload` | Runtime class on `RawTransaction.payload` for encrypted builds (use with `instanceof`).                              | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `MIN_ENCRYPTED_TXN_GAS_UNIT_PRICE`   | The 200-Octa gas-unit floor constant.                                                                                | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `EncryptionKey`                      | Per-epoch BLS12-381 batch encryption key.                                                                            | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `DecryptedPlaintext`                 | Plaintext executable + decryption nonce wrapper.                                                                     | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `PayloadAssociatedData`              | AAD bound into each encrypted payload (sender + auth-key pairs).                                                     | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `ClaimedEntryFunction`               | Class form of `claimedEntryFunction` (alternative to the plain-object form).                                         | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`                                                          |
| `Transaction` / `AptosConfig`        | Tree-shakeable namespace class (alternative to `Aptos`) — see [Two ways to call the SDK](#two-ways-to-call-the-sdk). | `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk/transaction` (also re-exported from `@aptos-labs/ts-sdk`) |

`fetchAndCacheEncryptionKey({ aptosConfig })` is internal but useful in tests; it returns `{ key, epoch } | null`.

## Reference tests

The TypeScript SDK exercises encrypted builds and submission in **`tests/e2e/transaction/encryptedTransaction.test.ts`**. The repo ships a script that runs only that suite against a devnet-capable network:

```bash
APTOS_NETWORK=devnet pnpm e2e-encrypted
```

Useful environment variables:

| Variable                         | Purpose                                                                                   |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `APTOS_NETWORK`                  | Required; any value except `local` opts into the suite.                                   |
| `APTOS_NODE_API_URL`             | Point at a fullnode whose ledger exposes `encryption_key` (public devnet often does not). |
| `REQUIRE_ENCRYPTION_E2E=1`       | Fail (don't skip) if `encryption_key` is absent — useful in CI.                           |
| `RUN_ENCRYPTED_NON_LEGACY_E2E=1` | Opt into SingleSender (unified Ed25519) and encrypted-multisig execution cases.           |

Source: [`aptos-ts-sdk` on GitHub](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-ts-sdk/blob/main/tests/e2e/transaction/encryptedTransaction.test.ts).

## Further reading

- **[Encrypted Pending Transactions guide](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions)** — product-level overview and visibility, using the consumer-facing term for this feature.
- **[Aptos batch encryption (cryptography)](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-core/tree/main/crates/aptos-batch-encryption)** — scheme variants and implementation detail in `aptos-core` (optional for app developers).
